transcript:
Maya Chen (Amaro): Thanks for making time on 2026-02-02. I wanted to use this like a working QBR, not a slideshow, and spend most of the time on what is actually helping the regional clinic teams.
Alturas Health Champion (Customer): That is perfect. The good news is adoption is broad now. Teams like the visibility, and honestly the handoff discipline is better than it was two quarters ago. We are seeing fewer tasks disappear between departments.
Alturas Health Admin (Customer): From an admin perspective, rollout has been much easier than some of our other tools. The only recurring ask I keep hearing is around export flexibility for leadership reviews.
Maya Chen (Amaro): Is that mostly a presentation-layer ask, or are there decisions your leaders cannot make quickly enough with the current reporting format?
Alturas Health Sponsor (Customer): Mostly speed and polish. The information is there. We just spend extra time reshaping it for operating reviews, and that feels like avoidable admin work.
Alturas Health Champion (Customer): Exactly. This is not a churn issue or even a major complaint. It is more that if reporting and exports got better, we would put the platform in front of even more leaders because it already has the trust.
Alturas Health Admin (Customer): Sorry, one nuance there. It would also help with winter surge planning, because right now we do a little manual cleanup before sharing dashboards with the regional operations group.
Maya Chen (Amaro): That is helpful. So the sharper version is not just prettier exports, it is less prep work for exec reviews and easier operational planning when staffing gets tight.
Nadia Park (Amaro): I like hearing that because it means the request is attached to success, not dissatisfaction. Those are often the best roadmap signals for us.
Alturas Health Champion (Customer): Small tangent, but it came up in our leadership meeting that the reason this rollout worked is the regional directors actually used it instead of delegating it downward. That is not always true for us.
Maya Chen (Amaro): That lines up with what we see in the best customers. The teams that pull leaders into the workflow early usually get much broader adoption than the ones that treat the system as admin-only.
Alturas Health Sponsor (Customer): If the reporting roadmap keeps moving, I would be comfortable being a reference for the product. The team genuinely likes what this has changed operationally.

transcript:
Priya Shah (Amaro): Thanks for making time on 2026-02-02. I wanted this one to be half roadmap, half rollout planning, because it feels like your team is already beyond the basic adoption questions.
Northstar Retail Group Champion (Customer): That is right. The product is working well for us. The conversation now is really about how much faster we can scale it to more stores without creating friction for regional managers.
Northstar Retail Group Admin (Customer): The two recurring asks I hear are better mobile approvals and more flexible permissions. Neither one is a deal problem, but both would reduce the amount of training and exception handling we are doing.
Priya Shah (Amaro): When you say mobile approvals, is the issue speed, screen design, or connectivity in the stores?
Northstar Retail Group Champion (Customer): Mostly speed and simplicity. Store managers are moving, they are interrupted constantly, and if the approval flow takes too much attention they postpone it until later.
Northstar Retail Group Sponsor (Customer): That is why I keep treating this as an expansion lever, not a complaint. We already trust the platform. We just want the last mile to be smoother for people in stores.
Northstar Retail Group Admin (Customer): Sorry, and the permissions point matters more than it sounds. Regional leaders want enough control to move quickly, but not so much that we end up with inconsistent process by market.
Priya Shah (Amaro): That is helpful. So the product ask is really a combination of faster mobile approvals and more practical role-based templates for regional leadership.
Nadia Park (Amaro): That is a strong signal for us because it is tied directly to rollout scale. Requests from healthy customers often tell us where the product can unlock the next wave of adoption.
Northstar Retail Group Champion (Customer): Small tangent, but one reason this matters is seasonal labor planning. During launch periods, managers are already coaching new staff and they have almost no spare attention.
Priya Shah (Amaro): That tracks with what we hear from other retail operators. The teams that make approvals almost invisible tend to get much better store-level follow-through.
Northstar Retail Group Sponsor (Customer): If you make progress on those two areas, I think the expansion conversation gets easier very quickly. Internally, this is already one of the better-behaved systems we use.

transcript:
Elena Torres (Amaro): Thanks for making time on 2026-02-02. I wanted to use this like a light QBR and talk through progress, blockers, and what the next quarter should look like. Maybe the best place to start is usage. I wanted to review adoption, open blockers, and how the team is feeling about the rollout.
BluePeak Logistics Champion (Customer): Yeah, overall people still like task orchestration across depots, and we get good comments about easy exception tracking, but we keep running into would like more native carrier integrations. In our world that usually shows up when a depot team is trying to keep exceptions moving without bouncing between systems.
BluePeak Logistics Admin (Customer): From the admin side, I would say some reporting friction also creates extra cleanup for us. It is not catastrophic, just repetitive. We end up doing more manual cleanup than we expected.
Elena Torres (Amaro): That makes sense. I want to understand whether this is mostly process friction, product friction, or some combination of both.
BluePeak Logistics Champion (Customer): It is more of a polish issue than a real blocker. The team is in a good place overall, and the main ask is just better integrations.
BluePeak Logistics Admin (Customer): Sorry, let me jump in there for a second. The practical issue is that frontline users do not describe this as one bug. They describe it as a lot of little speed bumps that pile up.
BluePeak Logistics Sponsor (Customer): From my side, the value case is still solid. I mostly want to know whether the product is evolving in the areas we care about.
Elena Torres (Amaro): That is helpful. We have heard similar asks around integrations, and I want to capture the sharpest version of the request from your side, not the polite version.
BluePeak Logistics Admin (Customer): The cleanest way to say it is this: The feature request that would make the biggest difference is better integrations, because that is what comes up in internal feedback. That is the thing our team keeps writing in notes after meetings.
Nadia Park (Amaro): I joined for this section because I wanted to hear it directly. It sounds like the request is not just convenience, it changes rollout confidence and admin overhead. I also hear that it affects how credible the next expansion conversation feels.
BluePeak Logistics Champion (Customer): Small tangent, but it came up in our leadership meeting: we spent a lot of time internally talking about dock scheduling and driver retention, because it affects how much operational change the teams can realistically absorb.
Elena Torres (Amaro): Understood. On our side, we have been discussing operational maturity benchmarks in logistics with similar customers, so I can bring a few concrete rollout ideas next time instead of keeping this abstract.
BluePeak Logistics Sponsor (Customer): If we can keep the roadmap discussion moving, I think we can broaden usage.

transcript:
Elena Torres (Amaro): Thanks for joining on 2026-02-02. I know this is a renewal-risk conversation more than a routine check-in, so I want to be pretty direct about what has to change in the next few weeks.
Redwood Bank Champion (Customer): That is fair. The team still sees value in the platform, especially around audit review workflows, but the mood has definitely shifted. People are asking whether we are adapting our process to the product instead of the other way around.
Redwood Bank Admin (Customer): From the admin side, the pain is not one dramatic outage. It is the steady accumulation of friction. Permissions are too coarse, audit questions take too long to answer, and I am still hand-building exports for control reviews.
Elena Torres (Amaro): When that comes up internally, is the concern mainly admin effort, or is it affecting confidence from operations leadership and compliance as well?
Redwood Bank Sponsor (Customer): It is both. If my controls team asks who changed a workflow state and we cannot answer that quickly, it stops being a product preference issue and becomes a governance issue.
Redwood Bank Champion (Customer): Honestly, this is the thing people keep bringing back to me. The biggest blocker right now is deeper audit logs, and yes, that is now part of the renewal discussion.
Redwood Bank Admin (Customer): Sorry, let me jump in. It is not even just audit logs in isolation. If roles were more granular, a lot of the internal objections would soften because the operational model would feel safer.
Elena Torres (Amaro): That helps. So if I say the combined ask is better audit logs, more granular role-based permissions, and less manual reporting work, does that capture the real blocker?
Redwood Bank Admin (Customer): Yes. If you asked me for the blunt version, we need better audit logs and compliance support before I can credibly tell the admins to expand usage. Right now they think we are signing up for more cleanup.
Nadia Park (Amaro): That is exactly why I joined. I do not want this summarized as a generic reporting ask if the real issue is operational trust. The difference matters for prioritization.
Redwood Bank Sponsor (Customer): Quick tangent, but this came up in an audit steering meeting yesterday. Everyone is already carrying extra change-control work because of upcoming exams, so tolerance for product friction is unusually low right now.
Elena Torres (Amaro): That context helps. We have been talking with other regulated customers about procurement risk framing and regulatory benchmarks, and I think I should bring you a tighter mitigation plan instead of another broad roadmap answer.
Redwood Bank Champion (Customer): That would be useful. I can handle hearing "not yet" on a feature if I also have a clear workaround, owner, and timeline. What is hard is carrying ambiguity back into the room.
Redwood Bank Sponsor (Customer): If we leave this with named owners and dates, I can keep the renewal conversation constructive. If not, I should be honest that it will probably get harder from here.

transcriptSegments.text: That is fair. The team still sees value in the platform, especially around audit review workflows, but the mood has definitely shifted. People are asking whether we are adapting our process to the product instead of the other way around.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Redwood Bank Admin
transcriptSegments.text: From the admin side, the pain is not one dramatic outage. It is the steady accumulation of friction. Permissions are too coarse, audit questions take too long to answer, and I am still hand-building exports for control reviews.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Serena Patel
transcriptSegments.text: When that comes up internally, is the concern mainly admin effort, or is it affecting confidence from operations leadership and compliance as well?

transcriptSegments.speaker: Redwood Bank Sponsor
transcriptSegments.text: It is both. If my controls team asks who changed a workflow state and we cannot answer that quickly, it stops being a product preference issue and becomes a governance issue.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Redwood Bank Champion
transcriptSegments.text: Honestly, this is the thing people keep bringing back to me. The biggest blocker right now is deeper audit logs, and yes, that is now part of the renewal discussion.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Redwood Bank Admin
transcriptSegments.text: Sorry, let me jump in. It is not even just audit logs in isolation. If roles were more granular, a lot of the internal objections would soften because the operational model would feel safer.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Serena Patel
transcriptSegments.text: That helps. So if I say the combined ask is better audit logs, more granular role-based permissions, and less manual reporting work, does that capture the real blocker?

transcriptSegments.speaker: Redwood Bank Admin
transcriptSegments.text: Yes. If you asked me for the blunt version, we need better audit logs and compliance support before I can credibly tell the admins to expand usage. Right now they think we are signing up for more cleanup.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Evan Brooks
transcriptSegments.text: That is exactly why I joined. I do not want this summarized as a generic reporting ask if the real issue is operational trust. The difference matters for prioritization.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Redwood Bank Sponsor
transcriptSegments.text: Quick tangent, but this came up in an audit steering meeting yesterday. Everyone is already carrying extra change-control work because of upcoming exams, so tolerance for product friction is unusually low right now.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Serena Patel
transcriptSegments.text: That context helps. We have been talking with other regulated customers about procurement risk framing and regulatory benchmarks, and I think I should bring you a tighter mitigation plan instead of another broad roadmap answer.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Redwood Bank Champion
transcriptSegments.text: That would be useful. I can handle hearing "not yet" on a feature if I also have a clear workaround, owner, and timeline. What is hard is carrying ambiguity back into the room.

transcriptSegments.speaker: Redwood Bank Sponsor
transcriptSegments.text: If we leave this with named owners and dates, I can keep the renewal conversation constructive. If not, I should be honest that it will probably get harder from here.

transcript:
Isaac Romero (Amaro): Thanks for making time on 2026-02-02. I was hoping we could do a practical adoption review today and separate signal from noise a bit. Maybe the best place to start is usage. I wanted to review adoption, open blockers, and how the team is feeling about the rollout.
Solstice Media Champion (Customer): Yeah, overall people still like campaign handoff visibility, and we get good comments about cross-team accountability, but we keep running into mobile workflow approvals are clunky. In our world that usually shows up when a campaign team is trying to move quickly without losing handoff visibility.
Solstice Media Admin (Customer): From the admin side, I would say needs easier dashboard exports also creates extra cleanup for us. It is not catastrophic, just repetitive. We end up doing more manual cleanup than we expected.
Isaac Romero (Amaro): That makes sense. I want to understand whether this is mostly process friction, product friction, or some combination of both.
Solstice Media Champion (Customer): It is not a fire, but it does keep slowing the teams down. We are seeing some pressure around mobile workflow approvals are clunky, but it still feels fixable if we keep momentum.
Solstice Media Admin (Customer): Sorry, let me jump in there for a second. The practical issue is that frontline users do not describe this as one bug. They describe it as a lot of little speed bumps that pile up.
Solstice Media Sponsor (Customer): From my side, the value case is still solid. I mostly want to know whether the product is evolving in the areas we care about.
Isaac Romero (Amaro): That is helpful. We have heard similar asks around mobile experience, reporting and exports, workflow automation, and I want to capture the sharpest version of the request from your side, not the polite version.
Solstice Media Admin (Customer): The cleanest way to say it is this: The feature request that would make the biggest difference is better mobile experience, because that is what comes up in internal feedback. That is the thing our team keeps writing in notes after meetings.
Nadia Park (Amaro): I joined for this section because I wanted to hear it directly. It sounds like the request is not just convenience, it changes rollout confidence and admin overhead. I also hear that it affects how credible the next expansion conversation feels.
Solstice Media Champion (Customer): Quick tangent, because it is related to how much change we can absorb: we spent a lot of time internally talking about content calendars, agency coordination, and ad budget shifts, because it affects how much operational change the teams can realistically absorb.
Isaac Romero (Amaro): Understood. On our side, we have been discussing storytelling around campaign planning with similar customers, so I can bring a few concrete rollout ideas next time instead of keeping this abstract.
Solstice Media Sponsor (Customer): If we can keep the roadmap discussion moving, I think we can broaden usage.

IronCore Manufacturing Admin (Customer): From the admin side, I would say would like no-code automations also creates extra cleanup for us. It is not catastrophic, just repetitive. We end up doing more manual cleanup than we expected.

transcript:
Nolan Brooks (Amaro): Thanks for making time on 2026-02-02. I wanted this to be an escalation follow-up, but also a bit of a reset, because the pattern across the last few calls is pretty clear.
Vanta Freight Champion (Customer): I appreciate that. Dispatch still likes the visibility when the integrations are working, but confidence is slipping because every manual workaround turns into another thing shift leads have to remember.
Vanta Freight Admin (Customer): That is exactly it. We are not mad about one broken feature. We are tired of brittle automations, partial carrier data, and then having to clean up reports for operations every week.
Nolan Brooks (Amaro): If we fixed only one thing first, would you put integrations ahead of automation reliability, or are those too connected to separate?
Vanta Freight Champion (Customer): They are connected, but integrations come first. If dispatch does not trust the incoming data, nobody really cares how elegant the automation rules are after that.
Vanta Freight Sponsor (Customer): From my side, the cost is credibility. We bought this to reduce operational drag. If the teams think it creates another reconciliation step, that is the narrative I have to unwind.
Vanta Freight Admin (Customer): Actually, before we move on, I want to be explicit about something. The request we keep making is not abstract integration coverage. We need the existing carrier flows to be dependable enough that dispatch is not second-guessing them mid-shift.
Nolan Brooks (Amaro): That is a good distinction. So the sharpest request is deeper and more reliable integrations first, then better workflow automation and reporting once trust in the underlying data improves.
Vanta Freight Admin (Customer): Yes. If you need a one-line version for product, here it is: make the carrier integrations trustworthy enough that my team stops building safety nets outside the platform.
Nadia Park (Amaro): That is very clear. I would much rather hear that directly than translate a softer request and miss the actual severity.
Vanta Freight Champion (Customer): Quick tangent, but this connects to how dispatch works. When fuel prices swing and driver availability gets weird, nobody has the patience for another manual checkpoint. That is when trust breaks fastest.
Nolan Brooks (Amaro): That makes sense. We have heard similar stories from other freight teams, especially around exception handling during high-volume weeks, so I should bring back a reliability-first plan rather than a broad innovation pitch.
Vanta Freight Sponsor (Customer): That is the right tone. Right now we do not need a vision deck. We need confidence that the core flow will hold under pressure.

transcript:
Maya Chen (Amaro): Thanks for making time on 2026-02-02. I wanted to use this like a light QBR and talk through progress, blockers, and what the next quarter should look like. Maybe the best place to start is usage. I wanted to review adoption, open blockers, and how the team is feeling about the rollout.
Oakwell Capital Champion (Customer): Yeah, overall people still like executive visibility into portfolio operations, and we get good comments about strong reporting for portfolio reviews, but we keep running into would like more flexible board-ready export formatting. In our world that usually shows up when an operating partner wants a board-ready answer without a lot of cleanup.
Oakwell Capital Admin (Customer): From the admin side, I would say some reporting friction also creates extra cleanup for us. It is not catastrophic, just repetitive. We end up doing more manual cleanup than we expected.
Maya Chen (Amaro): That makes sense. I want to understand whether this is mostly process friction, product friction, or some combination of both.
Oakwell Capital Champion (Customer): It is more of a polish issue than a real blocker. The team is in a good place overall, and the main ask is just better reporting and exports.
Oakwell Capital Admin (Customer): Sorry, let me jump in there for a second. The practical issue is that frontline users do not describe this as one bug. They describe it as a lot of little speed bumps that pile up.
Oakwell Capital Sponsor (Customer): From my side, the value case is still solid. I mostly want to know whether the product is evolving in the areas we care about.
Maya Chen (Amaro): That is helpful. We have heard similar asks around reporting and exports, and I want to capture the sharpest version of the request from your side, not the polite version.
Oakwell Capital Admin (Customer): The cleanest way to say it is this: The feature request that would make the biggest difference is better reporting and exports, because that is what comes up in internal feedback. That is the thing our team keeps writing in notes after meetings.
Nadia Park (Amaro): I joined for this section because I wanted to hear it directly. It sounds like the request is not just convenience, it changes rollout confidence and admin overhead. I also hear that it affects how credible the next expansion conversation feels.
Oakwell Capital Champion (Customer): Small tangent, but it came up in our leadership meeting: we spent a lot of time internally talking about board prep, portfolio oversight, and value-creation plans, because it affects how much operational change the teams can realistically absorb.
Maya Chen (Amaro): Understood. On our side, we have been discussing portfolio operations and operating partner playbooks with similar customers, so I can bring a few concrete rollout ideas next time instead of keeping this abstract.
Oakwell Capital Sponsor (Customer): If we can keep the roadmap discussion moving, I think we can broaden usage.
